content.problemStatement:
There are NNN frogs (numbered 111 through NNN) in a line. For each valid iii, the iii-th frog is initially at the position iii, it has weight WiW_iWi​, and whenever you hit its back, it jumps a distance LiL_iLi​ to the right, i.e. its position increases by LiL_iLi​. The weights of the frogs are pairwise distinct.

You can hit the back of each frog any number of times (possibly zero, not necessarily the same for all frogs) in any order. The frogs do not intefere with each other, so there can be any number of frogs at the same time at each position.

Your task is to sort the frogs in the increasing order of weight using the smallest possible number of hits. In other words, after all the hits are performed, then for each pair of frogs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that Wi<WjW_i \lt W_jWi​<Wj​, the position of the iii-th frog should be strictly smaller than the position of the jjj-th frog. Find the smallest number of hits needed to achieve such a state.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers W1,W2,…,WNW_1, W_2, \ldots, W_NW1​,W2​,…,WN​.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers L1,L2,…,LNL_1, L_2, \ldots, L_NL1​,L2​,…,LN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the smallest number of times you need to hit the backs of the frogs.
constraints: 2 ≤ N ≤ 2·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5)
1 ≤ Wi ≤ N and all Wi are distinct
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then for each test case:
- A line with integer N, the number of frogs.
- A line with N space‑separated integers W1 … WN, the distinct weights of the frogs.
- A line with N space‑separated integers L1 … LN, the jump lengths of the frogs.
Frog i initially stands at position i (1‑based).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum total number of hits required so that after all jumps the frogs are ordered strictly by increasing weight (lighter frog strictly left of heavier frog).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We can hit the back of the first frog three times.
Example case 2: We can hit the back of the first frog four times, then hit the back of the second frog two times.

solution.approach: Sort the frogs by weight in increasing order. Process them in this order while keeping the greatest final position achieved so far (prevPos). For the current frog i (original index i, jump length Li) its current position is i. If i > prevPos we need no hit. Otherwise we must increase its position until it becomes strictly larger than prevPos. Each hit adds Li, so the minimal number of hits needed is floor((prevPos - i) / Li) + 1. Update the position, add the hits to the answer and set prevPos to the new position. This greedy choice is optimal because any solution must move each frog at least enough to be to the right of all lighter frogs, and moving it more would only increase the total number of hits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of sorting; the simulation itself is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing weights, jumps and the order array.
